result.markdown:
--- title: "Why I Switched to Polars From Pandas" subtitle: "Benchmarking Polars vs Pandas DataFrames" author: "Russ Zaliznyak" date: "2025-10-21" description: > If you use Pandas DataFrames to store and analyze large datasets, consider switching to Polars. Benchmarks across read, write, construct, pivot, join, and filter — Polars wins every time. categories: [Python, Polars, Pandas, Performance] execute: echo: false --- # TL/DR; If you use **`Pandas`** DataFrames to store and analyze large datasets, consider making a change to **`Polars`**. I use _DataFrames_ because they allow me to think relationally --- just like I do when I write SQL. I use them extensively in virtually all of my work; app development, exploratory data analysis, and summarizing large datasets. Check out below the benchmarks I measured for typical DataFrame operations like read, write, construct, and group by. These will convince you that a change is necessary. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="System & Environment"} - All benchmarks ran on a **16-inch MacBook Pro (2021)** equipped with an **Apple M1 Max** chip and **64 GB of unified memory**, running **macOS Sonoma 14.7**.

- Tests used **Python 3.10.9**, with **Pandas 1.5.3** and **Polars 1.33.0** installed. ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Methodology"} I benchmarked **Polars** and **Pandas** across common DataFrame operations using both real and simulated data.

- For **read** and **write** tests, I used a **10 MB CSV dataset** from the [Frictionless Data repository](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/frictionlessdata/datasets/main/files/csv/10mb.csv).
- For **constructor**, **pivot**, **join**, and **filter** benchmarks, I generated a **simulated dataset** to ensure consistent structure and reproducibility.
- Each operation was executed **1\,000 times** per library, and the **mean execution time** was recorded. ::: # Read | Write [Download **10 mb file**: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/frictionlessdata/datasets/main/files/csv/10mb.csv](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/frictionlessdata/datasets/main/files/csv/10mb.csv){target=_blank} | Metric | Mean Speed Increase | Standard Deviation | |--------------|---------------------|--------------------| | Read | 18\.40 | 3\.20 | | Write | 39\.41 | 5\.85 | ::: {.callout-tip collapse="false" title="AI Generated Summary"} Polars is faster at reading and writing CSVs than Pandas because it's built in Rust with parallelized, memory-efficient algorithms that fully utilize multiple CPU cores. ::: # Construct | Pivot ::: {#a5d00b9a .cell execution_count=9} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Simulate Dataset"} import numpy as np import string import random N = int(5e6) def random_str(n=5): return ''.join(random.choices(string.ascii_letters, k=n)) data = { "id": np.arange(N), "value": np.random.randint(0, 1000, size=N), "flag": np.random.choice([True, False], size=N), "category": np.random.choice(["A", "B", "C", "D"], size=N), "score": np.random.randn(N), "year": np.random.randint(2000, 2025, size=N), "name": [random_str(5) for _ in range(N)], # random short strings "ratio": np.random.random(size=N), "count": np.random.randint(1, 100, size=N), } ``` ::: | Metric | Mean Speed Increase | Standard Deviation | |--------------|---------------------|--------------------| | Constructor | 1\.89 | 0\.14 | | Group By | 23\.99 | 1\.72 | ::: {.callout-tip collapse="false" title="AI Generated Summary"} **Constructor:** Polars DataFrame constructor is faster than Pandas because it uses Rust's zero-copy memory model and parallel data parsing, avoiding Python's object overhead. **Pivot Tables:** Polars outperforms Pandas in pivot table operations by executing grouped aggregations in parallel and leveraging an optimized query engine built on Apache Arrow for efficient columnar computation. ::: # Joins | Filter | Metric | Mean Speed Increase | Standard Deviation | |------------|---------------------|--------------------| | Inner Join | 2\.09 | 0\.52 | | Union All | 1\.32 | 0\.64 | | Filter | 1\.16 | 0\.36 |
::: {.callout-tip collapse="false" title="AI Generated Summary"} **Joins (Inner + Union All):** Polars performs joins faster than Pandas by using parallelized hash join algorithms implemented in Rust and operating directly on Arrow memory, minimizing copying and serialization overhead. **Filter Clauses:** Polars evaluates filter expressions lazily and in parallel at the columnar level, enabling vectorized, cache-efficient filtering that significantly outpaces Pandas' row-wise execution in Python. ::: # Conclusion Across the board `Polars` is faster at all of the typical DataFrame operations we tested. The syntax differences are minimal and AI can quickly convert your `Pandas` code. If you work with large DataFrames, Polars is worth the 30-minute migration — faster runtimes, smaller memory footprint, and minimal code changes. # Code References ::: { style="margin-top: -15px; font-size: 90%;"} ::: {.table-responsive} | Operation | **Pandas Example** | **Polars Example** | **Times Faster** | |------------|--------------------|--------------------|-----------------------| | **Read CSV** | `df = pd.read_csv("data.csv")` | `df = pl.read_csv("data.csv")` | 18\.40 | | **Write CSV** | `df.to_csv("out.csv")` | `df.write_csv("out.csv")` | 39\.41 | | **Constructor** | `df = pd.DataFrame(data)` | `df = pl.DataFrame(data)` | 1\.89 | | **Group By** | `df.groupby("col")["value"].mean()` | `df.groupby("col").agg(pl.col("value").mean())` | 23\.99 | | **Inner Join** | `df1.merge(df2, on="id", how="inner")` | `df1.join(df2, on="id", how="inner")` | 2\.09 | | **Union All** | `pd.concat([df1, df2], ignore_index=True)` | `df1.vstack(df2)` | 1\.32 | | **Filter** | `df[df["value"] > 10]` | `df.filter(pl.col("value") > 10)` | 1\.16 | ::: *** _Results will vary depending on machine and size/structure of your datasets._ ::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="**AI Generated Summary**: Why Union All Has High Variance "} Polars' `pl.concat()` (or `vstack()`) uses **multi-threaded Arrow buffer operations** that cut average runtime but introduce **non-deterministic variance**. On macOS with M1 Max chips, the OS may schedule threads differently across performance and efficiency cores, causing some runs to finish faster or slower. In short, **parallelism improves mean speed but naturally increases variance**—explaining the wider spread in the "Union All" benchmark. ::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="**AI Generated Summary**: Why Constructor is Reliably Faster "} The constructor benchmark is the most reliable because it's a **pure in-memory, CPU-bound operation** with no I/O, threading, or garbage collection noise. Each run follows the same predictable memory layout, making it a clean measure of Polars' and Pandas' internal efficiency without interference from system variability. :::
